title: Lemon Turkish delight
subtitle: Set lemon confection dusted with icing sugar and cornflour
excerpt: Lemon Turkish delight is a gelatine-and-starch set confection flavoured with lemon zest and juice, dusted thickly with icing sugar and cornflour. The set is firmer and cleaner than a purely starch-set lokum but keeps the characteristic bounce. It takes most of a day to make and set but only about 30 minutes of active work.
sourceNotes: Turkish delight (lokum) is documented in Ottoman culinary records from the eighteenth century and spread through Europe in the nineteenth century. The traditional starch-only set is a specialist technique requiring precise ratios and prolonged cooking. This recipe uses a combination of gelatine and cornflour, a widely adopted home-cook approach that gives a reliable firm set without specialist confectionery skills. The lemon flavour is a popular modern variant.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set overnight for best results. Dust in icing sugar and cornflour just before cutting and serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no baking required; sugar syrup is cooked on the hob

glossaryTerms.definition: In this recipe the set comes from both cornflour and gelatine. The cornflour is mixed with cold water and added to the hot sugar syrup where it thickens the mixture as it cooks. The gelatine, bloomed in cold water separately, is stirred in off the heat and provides the bouncy elasticity. The mixture sets in the tin over several hours. Adequate setting time is critical: if you cut it too soon it will be soft and sticky. Overnight refrigeration gives the firmest result.

content.text: The sugar syrup in this recipe reaches 115 degrees C. Keep children away from the hob while it is cooking. Do not stir the syrup once it has come to the boil, as this can cause crystallisation. Use a wet pastry brush to wash down any sugar crystals from the sides of the pan.
is what gives this delight its distinctive bounce. Set it overnight in the fridge for a clean cut. If the pieces are still soft after 6 hours, refrigerate for longer.

items.prepNote: about 6 to 8 leaves, soaked in cold water for 5 minutes

content.text: Oil a 20 cm square tin and line with baking paper. Dissolve the 400 g sugar in 300 ml water in a medium saucepan over low heat. Add the lemon juice and zest. Bring to the boil without stirring and cook to 115 degrees C, about 12 minutes. Mix the 60 g cornflour with 100 ml cold water until smooth. Add this mixture to the boiling syrup in a steady stream, whisking constantly. The mixture will thicken immediately. Cook over medium heat, stirring continuously, for 8 to 10 minutes until the mixture is very thick and a deep translucent colour.

Remove the pan from the heat. Squeeze the soaked gelatine leaves to remove excess water and stir them into the hot mixture until fully dissolved. Pour into the prepared tin and smooth the surface. Leave to cool at room temperature for 1 hour, then refrigerate for at least 6 hours or overnight. Mix the 40 g cornflour and 60 g icing sugar together in a shallow bowl. Dust a board generously with the mixture. Turn the set delight out, peel off the paper, and cut into 3 cm squares with an oiled knife. Toss each piece in the dusting mixture until well coated.

items.problem: Turkish delight is too soft to cut cleanly
items.solution: It was not set for long enough. Refrigerate for a further 4 to 6 hours. If still soft, the gelatine amount was insufficient; use 14 g next time.

items.problem: Sugar syrup crystallised in the pan
items.solution: The syrup was stirred after it came to the boil, or sugar crystals from the pan sides fell in. Use a clean brush dipped in water to wash down the sides. Do not stir once boiling.
